sentences.base_language: Camille had made a reservation at a new restaurant in the neighborhood that everyone had been raving about.
sentences.target_language: Camille avait réservé une table dans un nouveau restaurant du quartier dont tout le monde n'arrêtait pas de parler.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Avait réservé** — plus-que-parfait of **réserver** (auxiliary **avoir**): **avait réservé** (had made a reservation / had reserved). The plus-que-parfait situates this action before the story's main narrative time — the reservation was made before the evening described. Formation: imperfect of **avoir** or **être** + past participle. **Réserver une table** = to make a (table) reservation — more precise than **faire une réservation** in a restaurant context.
**Dont tout le monde n'arrêtait pas de parler** — **dont** as relative pronoun replacing **de** + antecedent: **un restaurant dont tout le monde parlait** (a restaurant that everyone talked about — lit. about which everyone talked). **Parler de** something → **dont** replaces **de** + noun in a relative clause. **N'arrêtait pas de + infinitif** = kept doing, couldn't stop doing: **n'arrêtait pas de parler** (kept raving about it / wouldn't stop talking about it). This construction with **arrêter de** in the negative expresses an ongoing or repeated action: **il n'arrête pas de pleuvoir** (it keeps raining).

sentences.base_language: When they arrived, the restaurant was beautifully decorated and already nearly full of diners.
sentences.target_language: À leur arrivée, le restaurant était joliment décoré et déjà presque plein de convives.
sentences.grammarNote:
**À leur arrivée** = when they arrived, upon their arrival: **à leur arrivée** (at their arrival). This compact prepositional phrase — **à** + possessive + verbal noun — replaces a full temporal clause (**quand elles sont arrivées**) with an elegant nominal construction very common in written and formal French. Compare: **à sa sortie** (upon leaving / when he left), **à leur retour** (when they returned).
**Était joliment décoré** — statal passive in the imparfait: **était décoré** (was decorated — describing a state, not an action). The imparfait expresses an ongoing background state at the moment of arrival. Compare with the dynamic passive in the passé composé: **le restaurant a été décoré** (the restaurant was decorated [at some point in time]). **Joliment** = beautifully, prettily — adverb formed from **jolie** (feminine of **joli**) + **-ment**: **jolie** → **joliment**. **Convive** (m./f.) = diner, fellow guest at table — a more refined synonym for **client** in a restaurant context.

sentences.base_language: A friendly waiter greeted them at the door and showed them to a lovely table near the window.
sentences.target_language: Un serveur aimable les a accueillies à la porte et les a conduites à une jolie table près de la fenêtre.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Les a accueillies / les a conduites** — direct object pronoun **les** placed before the auxiliary in the passé composé, with past participle agreeing with the feminine plural direct object. When the direct object pronoun precedes the verb in the passé composé, the past participle agrees in gender and number with that pronoun: **les** (referring to two women) → **accueillie**s, **conduite**s (feminine plural, adding **-es**). This agreement rule applies whenever the direct object precedes the verb — whether as a pronoun or in a relative clause: **la table qu'il a choisie** (the table he chose).
**Conduire quelqu'un à** = to show someone to, to lead someone to: **a conduites à une table** (showed them to a table). **Conduire** here is used figuratively (to lead, to escort) rather than in its most common meaning of 'to drive'. Compare: **accompagner** (to accompany), **mener** (to lead — slightly more formal). **Près de la fenêtre** = near the window — **près de** + noun indicates proximity: **près de** la porte (near the door), **près du** comptoir (near the bar/counter).

sentences.base_language: Camille felt immediately at ease — the atmosphere was elegant without being at all stiff.
sentences.target_language: Camille s'est tout de suite sentie à l'aise — l'atmosphère était élégante sans être le moins du monde guindée.
sentences.grammarNote:
**S'est sentie à l'aise** — pronominal verb **se sentir** (to feel) with **être** in the passé composé: **s'est sentie** (felt — feminine singular, past participle **sentie** agrees with the subject **Camille**, feminine). **Se sentir** + adjective or expression = to feel: **se sentir à l'aise** (feel at ease), **se sentir bien** (feel good), **se sentir seule** (feel lonely). The past participle of pronominal verbs agrees with the reflexive pronoun (which co-refers with the subject) when that pronoun is a direct object — here **se** is the direct object, so agreement applies.
**Sans être le moins du monde guindée** — **sans** + infinitif expresses the absence of a concurrent action or quality: **sans être** (without being). **Le moins du monde** = in the slightest, at all — a reinforced negation used after **sans**, **ne… pas**, or **ne… guère**: **sans être le moins du monde** guindée (without being at all stiff). **Guindé/e** = stiff, stilted, overly formal — used of a person, atmosphere, or style: **une ambiance guindée** (a stiff atmosphere), **un ton guindé** (a stilted tone).

sentences.base_language: The menu was long and impressive, with dishes that reflected both classic French technique and modern creativity.
sentences.target_language: La carte était longue et impressionnante, avec des plats qui reflétaient à la fois la technique française classique et la créativité moderne.
sentences.grammarNote:
**La carte** vs **le menu** — in French restaurant vocabulary, **la carte** is the full written menu listing all dishes and prices, while **le menu** refers to a set meal or fixed-price meal (e.g., a tasting menu or prix-fixe). To ask for the menu in the sense of the full list, say **la carte**: **je peux voir la carte ?** (Can I see the menu?). To order the set meal: **je prends le menu à 35 euros** (I'll have the set menu at 35 euros).
**À la fois… et** = both… and: **à la fois la technique… et la créativité** (both the technique… and the creativity). **À la fois** is placed before the first element of the pair; **et** links the second. Compare the English 'both… and' structure. **Reflétaient** — imparfait of **refléter** (to reflect): used here for an ongoing characteristic or quality that the dishes continuously displayed. The imparfait is the natural tense for describing enduring attributes in a past narrative context, as opposed to the passé composé which would imply a specific moment of reflection.

sentences.base_language: She decided on the tasting menu and asked the waiter for a wine recommendation.
sentences.target_language: Elle a opté pour le menu dégustation et a demandé au serveur de lui conseiller un vin.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Opter pour** = to opt for, to decide on: **a opté pour le menu dégustation** (decided on / opted for the tasting menu). **Opter pour** is slightly more deliberate and decisive than **choisir** (to choose) — it implies a considered selection among options: **opter pour une solution** (opt for a solution), **opter pour le silence** (choose silence). **Menu dégustation** = tasting menu — a fixed multi-course meal showcasing the chef's specialities: **le menu dégustation** is the standard term in French fine dining.
**A demandé au serveur de lui conseiller** — **demander à quelqu'un de + infinitif** = to ask someone to do something: **a demandé au serveur de conseiller** (asked the waiter to recommend). The structure is: **demander à** + person + **de** + infinitive. Note the indirect object **au serveur** (to the waiter) and the pronoun **lui** inside the infinitive clause, referring back to Camille (to recommend a wine for her / to her). **Conseiller un vin** = to recommend a wine: **conseiller** (to advise, to recommend) takes a direct object — the thing recommended.

sentences.base_language: He suggested a crisp Burgundy white wine that he said paired beautifully with the seafood starter.
sentences.target_language: Il a suggéré un vin blanc de Bourgogne vif et fruité qu'il a dit se marier à merveille avec l'entrée de fruits de mer.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Qu'il a dit se marier** — perception/declaration verb **dire** + infinitive in a relative clause: **qu'il a dit se marier à merveille** (that he said paired beautifully / which he said went wonderfully with). This construction — **dire** + infinitive — is used in reported speech to relay someone's claim about what something does: **il a dit ce vin se marier bien** is equivalent to reported speech where the original statement was **ce vin se marie bien avec…**. Compare: **qu'il a dit être** excellent (that he said was excellent).
**Se marier avec** = to pair with, to go well with (of food and wine): **se marier à merveille avec** (to pair beautifully with — lit. to marry wonderfully with). **Se marier avec** is the standard idiomatic expression for food and wine pairing in French: **ce vin se marie bien avec** le poisson (this wine pairs well with fish). **À merveille** = wonderfully, beautifully — adverbial phrase: **aller à merveille** (to go beautifully / to work wonderfully). **Vif** (of wine) = crisp, lively, fresh — a tasting term describing a wine with good acidity: **un vin vif** (a crisp wine).

sentences.base_language: The first course arrived — a delicate preparation of scallops with a light cream sauce and fresh tarragon.
sentences.target_language: La première entrée est arrivée — une délicate préparation de noix de Saint-Jacques avec une légère sauce à la crème et de l'estragon frais.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Est arrivée** — **arriver** takes **être** as auxiliary in the passé composé; past participle **arrivée** agrees with the feminine subject **la première entrée** (feminine singular, adding **-e**). The core group of verbs of movement and change of state take **être**: **arriver, partir, entrer, sortir, monter, descendre, naître, mourir, rester, tomber**, and all pronominal verbs. Past participle agreement with the subject is required for all **être** verbs.
**De l'estragon frais** — partitive article **de l'** before a masculine singular noun beginning with a vowel: **de l'estragon** (some tarragon / fresh tarragon). The partitive article (**du, de la, de l', des**) expresses an unspecified quantity of something uncountable. In the sentence, **de l'estragon frais** (some fresh tarragon) uses **de l'** because **estragon** is masculine and begins with a vowel. Note: after negation, all partitive and indefinite articles reduce to **de** (or **d'** before a vowel): **sans estragon** (without tarragon — no article after **sans**). **Noix de Saint-Jacques** (f.) = scallops — the standard French culinary term.

sentences.base_language: Camille took her first bite and felt a genuine surge of pleasure — it was one of the finest things she had ever tasted.
sentences.target_language: Camille a pris sa première bouchée et a ressenti une véritable montée de plaisir — c'était l'une des meilleures choses qu'elle eût jamais goûtées.
sentences.grammarNote:
**A ressenti** — passé composé of **ressentir** (to feel, to experience — an emotion or sensation): **a ressenti** (felt, experienced). **Ressentir** is used for internal experiences and emotions: **ressentir de la joie** (feel joy), **ressentir une douleur** (feel a pain). Compare: **sentir** (to smell, or to feel physically) vs **ressentir** (to experience emotionally or internally).
**Qu'elle eût jamais goûtées** — subjunctive plus-que-parfait in a superlative relative clause. After a superlative (**l'une des meilleures choses**), French literary style requires the subjunctive in the relative clause: **qu'elle eût jamais goûtées** (that she had ever tasted — subjunctive plus-que-parfait of **goûter**). In everyday spoken French, the indicative plus-que-parfait is also used: **qu'elle avait jamais goûtées**. Past participle **goûtées** agrees with the preceding direct object **qu'** (referring to **choses**, feminine plural). **Jamais** in a positive superlative context = ever (not 'never'): **la meilleure chose qu'elle ait jamais goûtée** (the best thing she has ever tasted).

sentences.base_language: The main course was a slow-cooked duck confit with sautéed seasonal vegetables and a red wine reduction.
sentences.target_language: Le plat principal était un confit de canard cuit à l'étouffée avec des légumes de saison sautés et une réduction de vin rouge.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Cuit à l'étouffée** = slow-cooked, braised (in a covered pot): **à l'étouffée** (cooked in a sealed vessel with steam — lit. 'smothered'). French culinary vocabulary is rich with specific cooking terms: **à l'étouffée** (braised/slow-cooked), **à la vapeur** (steamed), **au four** (oven-baked), **à la poêle** (pan-fried), **au bain-marie** (in a bain-marie). These expressions use **à** + definite article and are invariable.
**Des légumes de saison sautés** — indefinite article **des** before a plural noun: **des légumes** (some vegetables / seasonal vegetables). Note the word order: noun + adjective **sautés** (past participle used as adjective, placed after the noun as is typical for past participles used adjectivally). **De saison** = seasonal, in season — a post-nominal modifier: **légumes de saison** (seasonal vegetables). **Réduction** (f.) = reduction — a sauce concentrate made by simmering liquid until it thickens: **une réduction de vin rouge** (a red wine reduction) is standard culinary French. **Confit de canard** = duck confit — the leg and thigh of a duck slow-cooked in its own fat, a classic of French gastronomy.

sentences.base_language: As they ate, Camille and her friend talked and laughed, completely losing track of time.
sentences.target_language: En mangeant, Camille et son amie bavardaient et riaient, oubliant complètement la notion du temps.
sentences.grammarNote:
**En mangeant** — gérondif expressing simultaneity: **en mangeant** (as/while eating). The gérondif (**en** + present participle) indicates that talking and laughing happened simultaneously with eating. It is formed by taking the **nous**-form of the present tense, dropping **-ons**, and adding **-ant**: **mang-eons** → **mangeons** → (drop **-ons**) → **mangeant** → **en mangeant**. The three irregular gérondifs: **en étant** (être), **en ayant** (avoir), **en sachant** (savoir).
**Bavardaient et riaient** — imparfait used for two actions simultaneously ongoing in the background of the narrative: **bavardaient** (were chatting) and **riaient** (were laughing). The imparfait is the correct tense for concurrent, continuous background actions in past narration, as opposed to the passé composé which marks punctual or foregrounded events. **Bavarder** = to chat, to talk casually — slightly more informal than **parler**: **bavarder avec des amis** (chat with friends). **Oublier la notion du temps** = to lose track of time: **oublier la notion du temps** (to forget the notion of time / lose all sense of time) — a more literary expression than the colloquial **perdre la notion du temps**.

sentences.base_language: The waiter returned to check on them and Camille praised the food with great enthusiasm.
sentences.target_language: Le serveur est revenu s'enquérir d'elles et Camille a fait l'éloge des plats avec beaucoup d'enthousiasme.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Est revenu** — **revenir** takes **être** in the passé composé: **est revenu** (returned, came back — masculine singular, no extra ending). **Revenir** belongs to the family of movement verbs that take **être**: **venir, devenir, revenir, parvenir**. Compare: **est revenu** (came back — intransitive, **être**) vs **a rendu** (returned something — transitive, **avoir**).
**S'enquérir de** = to inquire about, to ask after, to check on: **s'enquérir d'elles** (check on them / ask how they were getting on). **S'enquérir de** is a formal/literary pronominal verb: **il s'est enquis de sa santé** (he asked after her health). In everyday speech, the same idea is expressed by **demander si tout allait bien** or **venir voir si tout se passait bien**. The register of **s'enquérir** reflects the formal, attentive tone of a professional waiter. **Faire l'éloge de** = to praise, to speak highly of: **a fait l'éloge des plats** (praised the dishes). **Faire l'éloge de** is more emphatic and formal than **complimenter** or **louer** — it implies enthusiastic, public praise.

sentences.base_language: He smiled and told them the chef would be delighted to hear that.
sentences.target_language: Il a souri et leur a dit que le chef serait ravi de l'apprendre.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Leur a dit** — indirect object pronoun **leur** (to them — plural) placed before the auxiliary: **leur a dit** (told them). **Leur** is the third-person plural indirect object pronoun, used for both masculine and feminine referents: **il leur a dit** (he told them), **elle leur a écrit** (she wrote to them). Compare: **lui** (singular — to him/her) vs **leur** (plural — to them). The indirect object pronoun always precedes the conjugated auxiliary in the passé composé.
**Que le chef serait ravi** — reported speech with tense shift: the original words were **le chef sera ravi** (the chef will be delighted — future), but in reported speech after a past main verb (**a dit**), the future shifts to the conditional: **serait ravi** (would be delighted). This tense concordance (concordance des temps) in reported speech: present → imparfait; future → conditional; passé composé → plus-que-parfait. **De l'apprendre** — infinitive with pronoun: **l'apprendre** (to hear/learn it — lit. to learn it). **Le** refers back to the praise Camille expressed. **Ravi/e de** + infinitive = delighted to: **ravi de l'apprendre** (delighted to hear it).

sentences.base_language: For dessert, Camille chose a classic crème brûlée, perfectly caramelized on top.
sentences.target_language: Pour le dessert, Camille a choisi une crème brûlée classique, parfaitement caramélisée sur le dessus.
sentences.grammarNote:
**A choisi** — passé composé of **choisir** (to choose): **a choisi** (chose). **Choisir** is a regular **-ir** verb (like **finir, grandir, réussir**): present **je choisis, tu choisis, il choisit**; passé composé **a choisi**. Past participle: **choisi** — no agreement here as there is no preceding direct object. Compare with a case requiring agreement: **la crème brûlée qu'elle a choisie** (the crème brûlée she chose — past participle **choisie** agrees with **qu'**, referring to feminine **crème brûlée**).
**Parfaitement caramélisée sur le dessus** — past participle used as an appositive adjective: **caramélisée** (caramelized) agrees with **une crème brûlée** (feminine singular, adding **-e**). An appositive past participle placed after a comma modifies the noun and must agree with it in gender and number: **une tarte bien garnie** (a well-garnished tart), **un soufflé bien gonflé** (a well-risen soufflé). **Sur le dessus** = on top — a spatial phrase: **sur le dessus** (on the top surface). **Crème brûlée** — a classic French dessert of custard with a caramelized sugar topping, the name itself meaning 'burnt cream'.

sentences.base_language: When the bill arrived, she was pleasantly surprised by how reasonable the price was for food of that quality.
sentences.target_language: Quand l'addition est arrivée, elle a été agréablement surprise de voir à quel point le prix était raisonnable pour une cuisine de cette qualité.
sentences.grammarNote:
**A été agréablement surprise** — passive voice in the passé composé: **a été** + past participle **surprise** (was surprised). The dynamic passive is formed with **être** conjugated in the target tense + past participle, which agrees with the subject: **elle** → **surprise** (feminine singular). Compare: **elle était surprise** (she was surprised — statal, imparfait) vs **elle a été surprise** (she was/got surprised — dynamic, passé composé marking the moment of being surprised).
**De voir à quel point** = at how (much), at the extent to which: **surprise de voir à quel point** (surprised to see how reasonable). **À quel point** is an interrogative phrase embedded in an indirect question meaning 'how much, to what extent': **tu ne sais pas à quel point** je suis content (you don't know how happy I am). **De voir** — **être surpris/e de** + infinitive = to be surprised to do: **surprise de voir** (surprised to see). Many adjectives expressing emotion take **de** + infinitive: **content de**, **heureux de**, **déçu de**, **surpris de**. **L'addition** (f.) = the bill (in a restaurant): **l'addition, s'il vous plaît** (the bill, please). Compare: **la facture** (invoice — commercial/formal), **la note** (bill — also used, especially in hotels).

sentences.base_language: They left a generous tip and thanked the staff sincerely before heading outside.
sentences.target_language: Elles ont laissé un généreux pourboire et ont remercié le personnel sincèrement avant de sortir.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Avant de + infinitif** = before doing: **avant de sortir** (before heading outside / before going out). **Avant de** + infinitive is used when the subject of both clauses is the same: **avant de partir** (before leaving), **avant de répondre** (before answering). When the subjects differ, use **avant que** + subjunctive: **avant qu'il parte** (before he leaves). This **avant de** vs **avant que** distinction is a key subjunctive trigger to master.
**Ont remercié le personnel** — **remercier quelqu'un** = to thank someone: **ont remercié le personnel** (thanked the staff). **Remercier** takes a direct object (the person thanked): **remercier le serveur** (thank the waiter), **remercier l'équipe** (thank the team). Compare: **remercier quelqu'un de/pour quelque chose** (to thank someone for something). **Le personnel** (m., collective) = the staff — always singular in French even though it refers to multiple people: **le personnel est** (the staff is/are). **Pourboire** (m.) = tip, gratuity — lit. 'for drinking' (**pour boire**): **laisser un pourboire** (to leave a tip).

sentences.base_language: Out on the pavement, both women agreed without hesitation that it had been a truly memorable evening.
sentences.target_language: Sur le trottoir, les deux femmes ont convenu sans hésiter que c'avait été une soirée vraiment inoubliable.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Ont convenu** — passé composé of **convenir** (to agree, to concur): **ont convenu** (agreed — both agreed / both concurred). **Convenir** takes **avoir** in the passé composé when used in the sense of 'to agree upon' or 'to suit': **nous avons convenu d'un rendez-vous** (we agreed on a meeting). Note: in the sense of 'to suit, to be suitable', **convenir** can take **être**: **cela leur est convenu** is used in Belgian and Swiss French, but standard French prefers **avoir**.
**Que c'avait été** — reported speech: the original thought was **c'a été** / **c'était une soirée inoubliable** (it was an unforgettable evening). After a past verb of saying or thinking (**ont convenu que**), the passé composé shifts to the plus-que-parfait in reported/indirect speech: **c'a été** → **c'avait été** (it had been). This tense backshift — the passé composé → plus-que-parfait in reported speech — is a key feature of **concordance des temps** in French. **Sans hésiter** = without hesitation — **sans** + infinitive (same subject): **sans hésiter** (without hesitating). **Inoubliable** = unforgettable — formed from **in-** (negative prefix) + **oubliable** (forgettable, from **oublier**).

sentences.base_language: Camille's feet ached pleasantly — they had walked for nearly an hour through the old quarter before dinner.
sentences.target_language: Camille avait les pieds qui lui faisaient agréablement mal — elles avaient marché pendant près d'une heure dans le vieux quartier avant le dîner.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Avait les pieds qui lui faisaient mal** — the construction **avoir** + body part + **qui** + verb describes a physical sensation: **avait les pieds qui faisaient mal** (had aching feet / her feet were aching). This idiomatic structure is preferred over **ses pieds lui faisaient mal**, which is grammatically correct but less natural. **Faire mal** = to hurt, to ache: **ça fait mal** (it hurts), **les pieds qui font mal** (aching feet). The indirect object pronoun **lui** (to her) indicates whose feet are aching.
**Avaient marché pendant près d'une heure** — plus-que-parfait expressing an action completed before a past reference point: **avaient marché** (had walked). **Pendant** + duration = for (a completed period of time): **pendant près d'une heure** (for nearly an hour). Compare: **depuis** + duration (action still ongoing at the reference point) vs **pendant** + duration (action fully completed within that period). **Près de** + number = nearly, almost: **près d'une heure** (nearly an hour), **près de vingt minutes** (nearly twenty minutes). **Le vieux quartier** = the old quarter — in French city geography, many cities have a historic **vieux quartier** (old quarter/district).

sentences.base_language: They decided to take a taxi home rather than walk, too content and full to rush anything.
sentences.target_language: Elles ont décidé de rentrer en taxi plutôt que de marcher, trop satisfaites et repues pour vouloir se presser.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Rentrer en taxi** = to go home by taxi: **rentrer** (to go back home — specifically returning to one's residence) + **en taxi** (by taxi). **En** + means of transport: **en taxi** (by taxi), **en voiture** (by car), **en métro** (by metro). Compare: **à pied** (on foot — using **à**, not **en**), **à vélo** (by bike). **Rentrer** vs **retourner**: **rentrer** implies returning to one's home or base; **retourner** means to go back to a place previously visited.
**Plutôt que de + infinitif** = rather than doing: **plutôt que de marcher** (rather than walking). **Plutôt que de** + infinitive expresses a preference by contrast: **rester plutôt que de partir** (stay rather than leave). When the subject is the same for both verbs, **plutôt que de** + infinitive is used. If subjects differ: **plutôt que + subjunctive**: **plutôt qu'il parte** (rather than have him leave).
**Trop satisfaites et repues pour vouloir se presser** — **trop + adjective + pour + infinitif** = too (adjective) to (do): too content and full to want to rush. **Repu/e** = full (after eating), satiated — specifically about being sated after a meal: **être repu** (to be full / replete). More specific than **rassasié** (satisfied — also used). **Se presser** = to hurry, to rush: **ne pas se presser** (not to rush), **pressez-vous !** (hurry up!). Compare: **se dépêcher** (to hurry — more colloquial).

sentences.base_language: In the taxi, Camille was already looking up the restaurant online, quietly planning her next reservation.
sentences.target_language: Dans le taxi, Camille cherchait déjà le restaurant en ligne, en préparant tranquillement sa prochaine réservation.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Cherchait… en préparant** — imparfait + gérondif expressing two simultaneous ongoing actions: **cherchait** (was looking up — imparfait for a background action in progress) and **en préparant** (while preparing — gérondif indicating a concurrent action). The gérondif here is equivalent to 'all the while planning': it describes what she was doing at the same time as the main action. The imparfait **cherchait** sets the scene; the gérondif **en préparant** adds a secondary simultaneous detail.
**En ligne** = online: **chercher en ligne** (to look up online / search online). **En ligne** is the standard expression for 'on the internet': **réserver en ligne** (book online), **trouver en ligne** (find online). Compare: **sur Internet** (on the Internet — also correct), **en ligne** (online — preferred in modern usage). **Tranquillement** = quietly, calmly, without fuss — adverb of **tranquille** (calm, quiet): **tranquille** → **tranquillement**. Here it conveys a sense of quiet satisfaction and contentment: she was already planning ahead, relaxed and happy. **Prochaine réservation** = next reservation — **prochain/e** before or after the noun: **la prochaine fois** (next time — before), **la semaine prochaine** (next week — after). Before the noun, **prochain** often implies the immediate next one.
